1: <header>The greenhouse gas emissions from meat sheep production contribute double of household consumption in a Eurasian meadow steppe </header>

3: The GHG from meat sheep production contribute double of household consumption in a Eurasian meadow steppe
Study of GHG emissions from Hulunbuir
4: <header>A R T I C L E INFO </header>
Keywords: Eurasian steppe Household ranches Life cycle assessment Structural equation modeling
5: With the rapid development of the economy, household activities have emerged as an important source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, making them a crucial focal point for research in the pursuit of sustainable development and carbon emission reduction. Hulunber, as a typical steppe region in eastern Eurasia, is repre- sentative of studying the GHG emissions from household ranches, which are the basic production units in this region. In this paper, based on survey data of 2018 and 2019, we quantified and assessed GHG emissions from household ranches by combining life cycle assessment (LCA) and structural equation modeling (SEM) ap- proaches, with LCA to define household ranches system boundary and SEM to determine the key driving factors of emissions. The results showed that GHG emissions of meat sheep live weight was 23.54 kg CO2-eq/kg. The

major contributor to household GHG emissions was enteric methane (55.23 followed by coal use (20.80 and manure management systems (9.16% and other contributing factors (14.81 The SEM results indicated that the GHG emissions from household ranches were derived primarily by economic level, while the economic level was significantly affected by income. This study also found a significant positive and linear correlation between household GHG emissions and the number of meat sheep (R2 = 0.89, P 0.001). The GHG emissions from meat sheep production (67.52 %) were double times greater than household livelihood consumption (32.48 These findings emphasized the importance of reducing emissions from meat sheep production and adjusting the energy mix of household livelihood, contributing to the establishment of a low-carbon household livelihood operation.

7: Continuous growth of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions has a sub- stantial effect on global warming, which poses a risk to the security of the world's food supply (Belanche et al., 2023). Over the last decade (2011-2020), global surface temperatures have increased by 1.09 °C compared to the Industrial Revolution era (1850-1900), and climate change has been occurring faster than in the last 1000 years (IPCC, 2021). Without proactive climate measures, global warming may cause temperatures to rise by >2.5 °C, which would result in a %-40 decrease in global per capita production, which implies serious cause for human and animal health (Burke et al., 2018; Iyer et al., 2022). With the pursuit of sustainable economic growth becoming a global consensus, climate change mitigation has become a key objective of global action (Mi et al., 2020). The goal of achieving carbon peaking by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060 is not only a core objective of China's climate change mitigation efforts, but also a necessary step in reducing global emissions (Meng et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023).

Globally, livestock production is estimated to account for between 9 % and 11 % of total anthropogenic GHG emissions, with about 44 of livestock emissions generated in the form of methane (CH4) (Chloe al., 2018). As the global population and wealth continue to grow and the demand for meat increases by 74 % in 2050, GHG emissions from livestock production will become a major concern of climatic change (Caro et al., 2014; Dangal et al., 2017). Livestock production not only provides food for people and regional food security, but also a source of livelihood for millions of people (Arvidsson Segerkvist et al., 2021; Mehrabi et al., 2020). However, the increasing resource consumption and environmental pollution from livestock production pose a threat to the sustainable development of the livestock husbandry (Xu et al., 2022). The carbon emissions from meat consumption have been gaining attention recently that the limiting the environmental impact of live- stock production has become a crucial issue, and numerous studies have been carried out to address this challenge globally (da Cunha et al., 2023; De Valck et al., 2023; Lauk et al., 2024; Ledgard et al., 2019).

China is the largest livestock producer globally, accounting for 22 of global meat production, and emissions reductions from livestock production were gradually gaining attention (Zhu et al., 2022). In recent years, several researchers explored the environmental impact of live- stock production and made remarkable contributions (Costa et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2019). Most of the studies conducted on the macroscale, including the national scale (Lin et al., 2015; Zhuang et al., 2019; Zhuang et al., 2020), urban scale (Luo et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2016) and regional scale of livestock production (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2018). However, to our knowledge, there is a lack of analysis on the pastoralist-level case. In addition, most of the studies focused on livestock production in agricultural and urban areas of China, and quite little is known about meat sheep production in pastoral areas (Guan et al., 2017; Tong et al., 2018). Furthermore, most of the studies primarily concentrated on emissions from livestock pro- duction, and ignored the emissions generated by the operators behind these activities and the comprehensive evaluation of household ranches. In this context, our study was conducted in the pastoral regions of Hulunber to address the key issues that have not been investigated in

Hulunber is an important livestock production base with abundant ranch resources in China. As the main economic form of local grassland utilization, the livestock production and household consumption behavior of household ranches have a direct impact on the development of climate change mitigation policies (Dong et al., 2023a; Dong et al., 2023b; Girod and De Haan, 2010). The meat sheep husbandry might contribute to the main role in the local economy and is a key factor for the survival and growth of the region's pastoralists. However, the meat sheep husbandry also had a substantial environmental impact that cannot be ignored. As a typical Eurasian meadow steppe, analysis of GHG emissions from Hulunber meat sheep husbandry is representative of household ranches in pastoral areas of China (Wu et al., 2022).

This study combined Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) with Structural equation modeling (SEM) to provide a comprehensive analysis of GHG emissions from household ranches. LCA is a methodology used to eval- uate environmental factors and their potential impact throughout the life cycle of a product (Baldini et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021b). In recent years, LCA has developed into a useful tool to measure the potential environmental performance of livestock production, providing more comprehensive information for environmental conscious consumers to choose sustainable products and production processes (McClelland et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021a). For instance, Biagetti et al. (2023) compared the role of the LCA method and EF in assessing the environ- mental benefits of milk production using the example of three cattle farms with different production intensities. By applying the LCA meth- odology, Kokemohr et al. (2022) assessed the environmental impact of three typical beef production cases in the European Union at the farm level. SEM which consists of the measurement and structural models is used to test and evaluate multivariate causality (Chen et al., 2015; Fan et al., 2016). Zhu et al. (2021) applied the SEM to analyze the rela- tionship between economy, livestock factors and land use in the Hulunber region of China. Measurement models measure latent or compound variables, while structural models test all hypothesized cor- relations based on path analysis (Zhu et al., 2021).

This study provided a comprehensive assessment of GHG emissions from household ranches in Hulunber by combining meat sheep pro- duction and household livelihood consumption. This study hypothesized that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production were higher than the household livelihood on household ranches and the two components were closely related in Hulunber. To test this hypothesis, a combination of LCA and SEM was used to quantify GHG emissions from household ranches. The main objectives of this study were to: (1) evaluate the GHG emissions per kilogram of live-weight meat sheep produced by house- hold ranches in Hulunber; (2) identify the key factors affecting GHG emissions from household ranches and provide suggestions for reducing emissions; (3) analyze the interrelationship between meat sheep pro- duction and household livelihood on household ranches of GHG emissions.

As a typical Eurasian meadow steppe, analysis of GHG emissions from Hulunber meat sheep husbandry is representative of household ranches in pastoral areas of China (Wu et al., 2022).

This study combined Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) with Structural equation modeling (SEM) to provide a comprehensive analysis of GHG emissions from household ranches. LCA is a methodology used to eval- uate environmental factors and their potential impact throughout the life cycle of a product (Baldini et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021b). In recent years, LCA has developed into a useful tool to measure the potential environmental performance of livestock production, providing more comprehensive information for environmental conscious consumers to choose sustainable products and production processes (McClelland et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021a). For instance, Biagetti et al. (2023) compared the role of the LCA method and EF in assessing the environ- mental benefits of milk production using the example of three cattle farms with different production intensities. By applying the LCA meth- odology, Kokemohr et al. (2022) assessed the environmental impact of three typical beef production cases in the European Union at the farm level. SEM which consists of the measurement and structural models is used to test and evaluate multivariate causality (Chen et al., 2015; Fan et al., 2016). Zhu et al. (2021) applied the SEM to analyze the rela- tionship between economy, livestock factors and land use in the Hulunber region of China. Measurement models measure latent or compound variables, while structural models test all hypothesized cor- relations based on path analysis (Zhu et al., 2021).

This study provided a comprehensive assessment of GHG emissions from household ranches in Hulunber by combining meat sheep pro- duction and household livelihood consumption. This study hypothesized that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production were higher than the household livelihood on household ranches and the two components were closely related in Hulunber. To test this hypothesis, a combination of LCA and SEM was used to quantify GHG emissions from household ranches. The main objectives of this study were to: (1) evaluate the GHG emissions per kilogram of live-weight meat sheep produced by house- hold ranches in Hulunber; (2) identify the key factors affecting GHG emissions from household ranches and provide suggestions for reducing emissions; (3) analyze the interrelationship between meat sheep pro- duction and household livelihood on household ranches of GHG emissions.
12: Continuous growth of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions has a sub- stantial effect on global warming, which poses a risk to the security of the world's food supply (Belanche et al., 2023). Over the last decade (2011-2020), global surface temperatures have increased by 1.09 °C compared to the Industrial Revolution era (1850-1900), and climate change has been occurring faster than in the last 1000 years (IPCC, 2021). Without proactive climate measures, global warming may cause temperatures to rise by >2.5 °C, which would result in a %-40 decrease in global per capita production, which implies serious cause for human and animal health (Burke et al., 2018; Iyer et al., 2022). With the pursuit of sustainable economic growth becoming a global consensus, climate change mitigation has become a key objective of global action (Mi et al., 2020). The goal of achieving carbon peaking by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060 is not only a core objective of China's climate change mitigation efforts, but also a necessary step in reducing global emissions (Meng et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023).

Globally, livestock production is estimated to account for between 9 % and 11 % of total anthropogenic GHG emissions, with about 44 of livestock emissions generated in the form of methane (CH4) (Chloe al., 2018). As the global population and wealth continue to grow and the demand for meat increases by 74 % in 2050, GHG emissions from livestock production will become a major concern of climatic change (Caro et al., 2014; Dangal et al., 2017). Livestock production not only provides food for people and regional food security, but also a source of livelihood for millions of people (Arvidsson Segerkvist et al., 2021; Mehrabi et al., 2020). However, the increasing resource consumption and environmental pollution from livestock production pose a threat to the sustainable development of the livestock husbandry (Xu et al., 2022). The carbon emissions from meat consumption have been gaining attention recently that the limiting the environmental impact of live- stock production has become a crucial issue, and numerous studies have been carried out to address this challenge globally (da Cunha et al., 2023; De Valck et al., 2023; Lauk et al., 2024; Ledgard et al., 2019).

China is the largest livestock producer globally, accounting for 22 of global meat production, and emissions reductions from livestock production were gradually gaining attention (Zhu et al., 2022). In recent years, several researchers explored the environmental impact of live- stock production and made remarkable contributions (Costa et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2019). Most of the studies conducted on the macroscale, including the national scale (Lin et al., 2015; Zhuang et al., 2019; Zhuang et al., 2020), urban scale (Luo et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2016) and regional scale of livestock production (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2018). However, to our knowledge, there is a lack of analysis on the pastoralist-level case. In addition, most of the studies focused on livestock production in agricultural and urban areas of China, and quite little is known about meat sheep production in pastoral areas (Guan et al., 2017; Tong et al., 2018). Furthermore, most of the studies primarily concentrated on emissions from livestock pro- duction, and ignored the emissions generated by the operators behind these activities and the comprehensive evaluation of household ranches. In this context, our study was conducted in the pastoral regions of Hulunber to address the key issues that have not been investigated in

Hulunber is an important livestock production base with abundant ranch resources in China. As the main economic form of local grassland utilization, the livestock production and household consumption behavior of household ranches have a direct impact on the development of climate change mitigation policies (Dong et al., 2023a; Dong et al., 2023b; Girod and De Haan, 2010). The meat sheep husbandry might contribute to the main role in the local economy and is a key factor for the survival and growth of the region's pastoralists. However, the meat sheep husbandry also had a substantial environmental impact that cannot be ignored. As a typical Eurasian meadow steppe, analysis of GHG emissions from Hulunber meat sheep husbandry is representative of household ranches in pastoral areas of China (Wu et al., 2022).

This study combined Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) with Structural equation modeling (SEM) to provide a comprehensive analysis of GHG emissions from household ranches. LCA is a methodology used to eval- uate environmental factors and their potential impact throughout the life cycle of a product (Baldini et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021b). In recent years, LCA has developed into a useful tool to measure the potential environmental performance of livestock production, providing more comprehensive information for environmental conscious consumers to choose sustainable products and production processes (McClelland et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021a). For instance, Biagetti et al. (2023) compared the role of the LCA method and EF in assessing the environ- mental benefits of milk production using the example of three cattle farms with different production intensities. By applying the LCA meth- odology, Kokemohr et al. (2022) assessed the environmental impact of three typical beef production cases in the European Union at the farm level. SEM which consists of the measurement and structural models is used to test and evaluate multivariate causality (Chen et al., 2015; Fan et al., 2016). Zhu et al. (2021) applied the SEM to analyze the rela- tionship between economy, livestock factors and land use in the Hulunber region of China. Measurement models measure latent or compound variables, while structural models test all hypothesized cor- relations based on path analysis (Zhu et al., 2021).

This study provided a comprehensive assessment of GHG emissions from household ranches in Hulunber by combining meat sheep pro- duction and household livelihood consumption. This study hypothesized that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production were higher than the household livelihood on household ranches and the two components were closely related in Hulunber. To test this hypothesis, a combination of LCA and SEM was used to quantify GHG emissions from household ranches. The main objectives of this study were to: (1) evaluate the GHG emissions per kilogram of live-weight meat sheep produced by house- hold ranches in Hulunber; (2) identify the key factors affecting GHG emissions from household ranches and provide suggestions for reducing emissions; (3) analyze the interrelationship between meat sheep pro- duction and household livelihood on household ranches of GHG emissions.
13: The Hulunber is located in the northeast of the Inner Mongolia and is a typical grassland with abundant natural pasture in China. As world- renowned natural pasture, the grassland animal husbandry is a char- acteristic advantageous industry in this region, which contributes significantly to the local economy and the livelihood of pastoralists (Han, 2020). We designed a questionnaire based on relevant informa- tion and selected Chenbaerhu Banner, Ewenk Autonomous Banner, Xeltala Ranch, and Tnihe Ranch in Hulunber (Fig. 1). The household surveys conducted in 2018 and 2019, spending 1 h per questionnaire and collected a total of 350 research questionnaires. The study focused on meat sheep production systems, and 160 sheep-raising households were selected in local household ranches. A stratified sampling method was used to ensure the selection of a diverse and representative set of households, effectively addressing the study's research questions. The survey was carried out in two groups, each consisting of a recorder and a bilingual local Mongolian guide to solve the language barriers during data collection. The Field surveys indicated that sheep production in was divided into grazing and captive periods according to local climatic conditions. According to the research, it is known that most of the ewes were lambed in March-April and the current year's lambs were sold in August-September. The survey mainly included basic household struc- ture (ethnicity, population, age, education level), energy consumption, grassland utilization, livestock breeding information (herd composition and number, slaughter information, sale price, feed purchase and other production materials), herding household income, expenditure and living infrastructure. The energy consumption in the region primarily relies on the burning of cow dung and raw coal, while the dietary structure (with a high proportion of meat) and lifestyle (largely dispersed) of the local pastoralists differ noticeably from those in rural

The functional unit in this study was 1 kg of live weight of meat sheep. The system boundary for meat sheep production was from the cradle to the farm gate, and the GHG assessment for household ranches included meat sheep production and household livelihood (Fig. 2). GHG emissions from meat sheep production in household ranches were associated with enteric methane, manure management systems, forage grain cultivation, and transportation. GHG emissions from household livelihood included direct and indirect energy consumption for light and heat, as well as non-energy product consumption. The GHG emissions calculation process for livestock production required an allocation with different methods, including allocation based on economic, mass, en- ergy, and protein content (Nguyen et al., 2012). The economic alloca- tion method was used in the current study.

The Global Warming Potential (GWP) was utilized to measure different GHG contributions, which is the most widely recognized measure of the impact of GHG on changing climate (Ibidhi et al., 2017; Zhuang et al., 2017). According to the IPCC (2013), GHG emissions were calculated by converting the corresponding GWP to carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2-eq). The GWP of CO2, CH4 and N2O were taken as 1, 28, and 265, over a 100-year horizon, respectively, and GHG emissions were expressed in GWP in kg CO2-eq.

17: GHG emissions from household ranches in this study were mainly from household livelihood consumption and meat sheep production. Household consumption had become an essential driver of rapid eco- nomic expansion, resulting in increased energy consumption and GHG emissions (Tian et al., 2016). Household GHG emissions result from the
Fig. 1. Study area and household ranches distributions.

Fig. 2. Overview and system boundaries of household ranches. The purple line and frame represented the household livelihood component, and the green and frame line represented energy, the red line represented GHG emissions. The blue line and frame represented on-farm production and the brown line and frame represented off-farm production activities, both of which were meat sheep production.

production of goods and services consumed by households and could be divided into direct and indirect emissions (Hubacek et al., 2009). Direct emissions were mainly from the direct use of energy (coal, gas, oil, electricity, and heat) for lighting, cooking, heating, and cooling, and indirect emissions were from the consumption of non-energy goods and services that fulfill basic needs (Ma et al., 2016). Methane and nitrous oxide emissions from ruminant respiratory metabolism, rumen fermentation, and manure management activities were substantial contributors to GHG emissions in livestock production (Zhuang et al., 2019). Methane and manure management system emissions from enteric fermentation in meat sheep were estimated according to the IPCC Tier 2 method (2019) adapted for Chinese conditions. Feed digestibility and various animal characteristics were used to calculate CH4 emission factors per animal for each age-sex category. Compared to the 2006 guidelines (IPCC, 2006), the 2019 guidelines (IPCC, 2019) were updated to revise some of the parameters for estimating enteric emissions and manure management emissions. This study utilized the LCA approach to establish the system boundary of household ranches and evaluated their direct and indirect GHG emissions. SEM was applied to analyze the drivers of GHG emissions from household ranches, including economic level and household features.

modities in their daily lives, resulting in GHG emissions associated with household consumption. These emissions could be divided into four categories: the production and laundering of clothing, the production and preparation of food, the construction of housing, and travel. EIndirectfEE (3)

where Nc was the number of clothes purchased per household, Nd was the number of detergents used, and EFc and EF were the CO2 emission factors for clothes and detergents, respectively. GHG from the production and processing of food. (5)

Nj was the consumption of food category j (including cereals, meat, milk, vegetables, and vegetable oil) and EFj was the corresponding CO2 emission factor. For housing, the carbon emissions of building pastoralist dwellings (usually brick structures) were calculated. GHG generated by pastoralist housing construction. (6)

N° refers to the consumption of material g during the construction of a unit area household; Area was the floor area of the house; EF was the CO2 emission factor of material g; and Year was the lifetime of the house. GHG from pastoralists' travel included emissions from traveling to towns to purchase necessities and from participation in social activities. The research questionnaire investigated the total transportation costs of pastoralists in 2018 and converted these costs into gasoline usage uni- formly. GHG emissions from travel pastoralists were calculated based on 2018 gasoline prices in the Hulunber region.
C was the cost of herding household travel in 2018, Yuan; 7.6 was the gasoline price in the Hulunber region in 2018; 0.725 was the gasoline density; and EF was the gasoline emission factor.

For housing, the carbon emissions of building pastoralist dwellings (usually brick structures) were calculated. GHG generated by pastoralist housing construction. (6)

N° refers to the consumption of material g during the construction of a unit area household; Area was the floor area of the house; EF was the CO2 emission factor of material g; and Year was the lifetime of the house. GHG from pastoralists' travel included emissions from traveling to towns to purchase necessities and from participation in social activities. The research questionnaire investigated the total transportation costs of pastoralists in 2018 and converted these costs into gasoline usage uni- formly. GHG emissions from travel pastoralists were calculated based on 2018 gasoline prices in the Hulunber region.
C was the cost of herding household travel in 2018, Yuan; 7.6 was the gasoline price in the Hulunber region in 2018; 0.725 was the gasoline density; and EF was the gasoline emission factor.

EFf and EFm were emission factors for enteric methane and manure management systems; VS was the daily volatile solid excretion of meat sheep [in dry matter, kg.(head-d)-1]; 365 was the base for calculating the annual VS production (d/a); B was the maximum methane pro- duction capacity of manure produced by meat sheep, m³ CH4/kg VS excreta; 0.67 was the factor for converting CH4 to kg CH4; MCF(s k) was the methane conversion factor (%) for each manure management system S in climate zone k, dimensionless. 25: <header>2.3.3. Emissions from supporting activities of meat sheep production </header>

Indirect GHG emissions in meat sheep production mainly result from growing fodder grain, including direct and indirect N2O emissions from nitrogen fertilizer application, diesel use in fall haying, fertilizer pro- duction, urea decomposition, electricity for irrigation, and trans- portation of agricultural materials. Tfeed = Tco2,hay TCO2,fer + TN20,N Tco2,urea + Tco2,feed TCO2,trans (11) Tfeed was GHG emissions from fodder cultivation and processing; Tco2,hay, Tco2,fer, Tco2,urea Tco2,fee and TCO2, trans are CO2 emissions from grazing diesel consumption, fertilizer production, urea application, other resource inputs, and pastures transportation, respectively, and TN20,N was N2O emissions from nitrogen fertilizer application. Details of the formulae are provided in the supplementary material.
26: This section presented the intensity and sources of GHG emissions and their proportions from meat sheep production on household ranches in the study area. SEM was applied to obtain the drivers of GHG emis- sions and explored the correlations between the factors.
27: <header>3.1. GHG emissions intensity of meat sheep for household ranches </header>

The results showed that the GHG intensity of the live weight of meat sheep produced was 23.54 kg CO2-eq/kg (Table 1). When considering emissions from the entire household ranches, the range of emissions of meat sheep produced was 10.96 to 163.24 kg CO2-eq/kg. The maximum emission values could be attributed to the fact that the household ranch had a high number of meat sheep, but fewer sheep were sold during the year, resulting in high emissions per unit of meat sheep. The minimum
Greenhouse gas emissions of 1 kg live weight of meat sheep on household ranches in Hulunber (kg CO2-eq).

Sources of emissions from different links of household ranches|Mean|Median|Min|Max GHG emissions from the live weight of 1 kg meat sheep|23.54|27.90|10.96|163.24 GHG emissions of 1 kg of meat sheep produced in the production process|16.44|18.12|8.50|89.70 Direct emissions from meat sheep production|15.68|16.61|7.94|79.74 Indirect emissions from meat sheep production|0.76|0.97|0|17.11 GHG emissions of 1 kg meat sheep in household livelihood|7.87|9.18|0.64|132.62 Direct emissions from the household livelihood|5.52|6.62|0.04|93.16 Indirect emissions from the household livelihood|2.35|2.61|0.38|61.57

value was mainly due to the fact that the household ranch had a low number of meat sheep but a high percentage of meat sheep sold in the current year. When only emissions from meat sheep production on household ranches were considered, the emissions of meat sheep were 16.44 kg CO2-eq/kg, with a range of emissions from 8.50 to 89.70 kg CO2-eq. In addition, the emissions from meat sheep were 7.87 kg CO2- eq/kg, with a range of emissions from 0.64 to 132.62 kg CO2-eq, if only the emissions from household livelihood were considered.

This study investigated the distribution of household numbers in terms of the intensity of meat sheep GHG emissions and the average household emissions (Fig. 3). The results showed that the largest group of households, with GHG emission intensities between 20 and 30 kg CO2-eq accounts for 31 % of all households (Fig. 3a). Additionally, 41 households fall into the range of 10-20 kg CO2-eq, while 22 households fall into the range of 30-40 kg CO2-eq accounting for 26 and 14 % of all households respectively. This indicated that the emission intensity of meat sheep production on most household ranches was in the range of 20-30 kg CO2-eq. Correspondingly, the average household GHG emis- sions from household ranches in the region were 46.83 t CO2-eq, but spanned a wide range from 1.82 to 188.57 t CO2-eq (Fig. 3b). The number of households ranches with GHG emissions ranging between 0 and 10 t CO2-eq was 35, which occupied the highest proportion of all households (22 %); that with GHG emissions ranging from 10 to 20 t CO2-eq was 24, constituting 15 % of the total number of households ranches. The remaining household ranches contributed <10 % to the average household GHG emissions.

31: <header>3.2. The sources of GHG emissions for household ranches </header>

The different components of GHG emissions from household ranches showed displayed substantial variations (Fig. 4). The total emissions from household ranches could be separated into meat sheep production and household livelihood emissions. Meat sheep production contributed 67.52 of total GHG emissions, while household livelihood accounted for 32.48 %, making the intensity of GHG emissions from meat sheep production twice that of household livelihood. The main sources of emissions were observed from enteric methane (55.23 9 followed by coal use (20.80 %) and manure management systems (9.16 %). The emissions of food consumption (7.73 %), haying diesel consumption (2.14%) and electricity (1.70 %) were in descending order, with the remaining production chain contributing <1 % of total emissions. The direct emissions from meat sheep production and household livelihood accounted for 64.39 % and 22.65 %, while the indirect emissions made up 3.13 % and 9.84 % of the total emissions, respectively. These two components could be further divided into direct and indirect emissions.
Among the emissions from the meat sheep production, the highest values (81.80%) were observed from the enteric methane followed by

34: <header>3.3. Influencing factors of GHG emissions household ranches </header>

SEM was utilized to analyze the complex interactions and feedback among the household GHG emissions, economy (ECON) and household features (HF) as well as the latent variables. The major variables in SEM represented the economic measures including gross household income (Income), number of vehicles (Vehicle), and pasture size (Pasture). The ratio of labor (Labor ratio), the ratio of males (Male_ratio), the house- hold size (Household size) and the age of the head (Head_age) in the household were used for household features measures. Hypothesized
Fig. 5. (a) the proportion of greenhouse gas emissions in the meat sheep pro- duction. (b) the proportion of greenhouse gas emissions in the household livelihood.

SEM for the changes in household GHG emissions, economy, and household features. Latent variables were denoted with circles and measured variables were represented with squares. A one-direction arrow indicates a hypothesized causal relationship between the two variables, while a two-way arrowed line indicates a feedback relationship.

The results showed that ECON had a stronger effect on GHG (0.72) than HF (0.09), and HF had no notable effect on the economic level (Fig. 6). The change in economic level was positively correlated with Income (0.97), Pasture (0.51) and Vehicle (0.19). The HF seemed to be more influenced by the Household size (-0.68) than Labor ratio (0.88),

Fig. 6. Hypothesized SEM for the changes in GHG (greenhouse gas), ECON (economy) and HF (household features). The blue solid line represented a sig- nificant impact, with the thickness of the line indicating the significance of the influence. The dashed line represented no significant impact.

Head_age (0.33) and Male ra (0.20). Furthermore, the study found a significant positive and linear correlation (R2 = 0.89, P < 0.001) be- tween household GHG emissions and the number of meat sheep. This suggested that an increase in the number of meat sheep is associated with a corresponding increase in household GHG emissions (Fig. 7).

37: <header>4.1. Comparison of emission intensity with other studies </header>

The GHG emissions per 1 kg live weight of meat sheep produced in our study were 23.54 kg CO2-eq, with ranging from 10.96 to 163.24 kg CO2-eq, which was in line with existing literature (Edwards-Jones et al., 2009; Ripoll-Bosch et al., 2013; Samardzió et al., 2020) (Table S2). For instance, Edwards-Jones et al. (2009) revealed that the emissions from meat sheep live weight of 8.1-31.7 kg CO2-eq/kg and 20.3-143.5 kg CO2-eq/kg in two farms in the UK, within a system including animal and farm soil GHG emissions. Samardzió et al. (2020) studied on GHG assessment of sheep farming in Serbia, which concluded ewes, rams, and lambs emitted on average 21.41, 19.13, and 3.20 kg CO2-eq per kg body weight, with system boundaries including primary reproduction pro- cesses and background processes. Different system boundaries were set in both studies and the variation in results was calculated, with the former considering emissions from farm soils and the latter analyzing different levels of emissions from different sheep species. Other studies had produced different results depending on the degree of intensifica- tion of farming. An example of this was the study carried out by Ripoll- Bosch et al. (2013), who assessed the three types of meat sheep farming in Spain with different degrees of intensification, and the GHG emissions per kg of live weight ranged from 19.5 to 25.9 kg CO2-eq. Besides, Dougherty et al. (2018) used different allocation methods, found that the carbon footprint of California market lamb production ranges from 13.9 to 30.6, 10.4-18.1, and 6.6-10.1 kg CO2 eq/kg when calculated by mass, economy, and protein quality, respectively.

Most of the above studies used 1 kg live body weight as the functional unit, and the system boundary was from the cradle to the farm gate, providing a standardized measurement for sheep research. However, the results obtained in different studies were difficult to compare because of the differences in system boundaries, used methods, functional units, allocation criteria, and the number of surveyed farms, but they still provided some valuable references. A preliminary comparison showed that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production in some developed European countries were at a low level, which was closely related to their mature production systems (Dougherty et al., 2018; Horrillo et al.,
Number of meat sheep on household ranches
Fig. 7. Regression relationship between the household greenhouse gas emis- sions and the number of meat sheep.

2020). As mentioned above, the emission ranges in this study were consistent with the results of several of these studies, but the results were relatively high, the main reason for this was that our study area was mostly small-scale farming, with the rough operation, backward tech- nology, insufficient labor, poor production conditions and low degree of intensification, which leads to the weaker ability of herders to cope with risks and higher GHG emissions. Meanwhile, it further causes over- loading to be common, leading to the deterioration of grassland ecological environment (Wang, 2022). Therefore, household ranches in the study area should focus on the implementation of business models that operates with an efficient combination of various factors of pro- duction, such as labor resources, pasture, capital, and information, to obtain optimal benefits and achieve the lowest GHG emissions. Mean- while, there were fewer studies on GHG emissions from meat sheep on household ranches in pastoral areas in China, and there was a broad research space.
38: <header>4.1. Comparison of emission intensity with other studies </header>

The GHG emissions per 1 kg live weight of meat sheep produced in our study were 23.54 kg CO2-eq, with ranging from 10.96 to 163.24 kg CO2-eq, which was in line with existing literature (Edwards-Jones et al., 2009; Ripoll-Bosch et al., 2013; Samardzió et al., 2020) (Table S2). For instance, Edwards-Jones et al. (2009) revealed that the emissions from meat sheep live weight of 8.1-31.7 kg CO2-eq/kg and 20.3-143.5 kg CO2-eq/kg in two farms in the UK, within a system including animal and farm soil GHG emissions. Samardzió et al. (2020) studied on GHG assessment of sheep farming in Serbia, which concluded ewes, rams, and lambs emitted on average 21.41, 19.13, and 3.20 kg CO2-eq per kg body weight, with system boundaries including primary reproduction pro- cesses and background processes. Different system boundaries were set in both studies and the variation in results was calculated, with the former considering emissions from farm soils and the latter analyzing different levels of emissions from different sheep species. Other studies had produced different results depending on the degree of intensifica- tion of farming. An example of this was the study carried out by Ripoll- Bosch et al. (2013), who assessed the three types of meat sheep farming in Spain with different degrees of intensification, and the GHG emissions per kg of live weight ranged from 19.5 to 25.9 kg CO2-eq. Besides, Dougherty et al. (2018) used different allocation methods, found that the carbon footprint of California market lamb production ranges from 13.9 to 30.6, 10.4-18.1, and 6.6-10.1 kg CO2 eq/kg when calculated by mass, economy, and protein quality, respectively.

Most of the above studies used 1 kg live body weight as the functional unit, and the system boundary was from the cradle to the farm gate, providing a standardized measurement for sheep research. However, the results obtained in different studies were difficult to compare because of the differences in system boundaries, used methods, functional units, allocation criteria, and the number of surveyed farms, but they still provided some valuable references. A preliminary comparison showed that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production in some developed European countries were at a low level, which was closely related to their mature production systems (Dougherty et al., 2018; Horrillo et al.,

41: <header>4.2. Different emissions sources in household ranches of this study compared with other studies </header>

As for key influencing factors, enteric methane was the main source of GHG emissions from household ranches in this study (55.23 %). O'Brien et al. (2016) and Cerri et al. (2016) also concluded that the enteric methane was the main contributor of GHG emissions from livestock production. This was also confirmed by the results of Wiede- mann et al. (2015), who evaluated the resource utilization and envi- ronmental impact of export lambs in Australia, and revealed that enteric methane contributed to %-89 of emissions, highlighting the need for enhanced sheep productivity to reduce GHG emissions intensity. Therefore, livestock production of enteric methane should be given sufficient attention.

The meat sheep production emissions were 2.08 times higher than the livelihood emissions consumption in household ranches. This study classified the GHG emissions into meat sheep production and household livelihood to provide a more visual examination of household ranches emissions. In the research region, GHG emissions from households' livelihood accounted for 32.48 % of the total emissions, while meat sheep production accounted for 67.52 %. In contrast to agricultural regions or urban areas, livestock production operations in pastoral areas played a crucial role in household ranches and were significant local economic source and material foundation. Although household liveli- hood emissions accounted for a smaller portion compared to production emissions, they still represented an important source of emissions for households (Mi et al., 2020). Economic development and rising living standards of pastoralists have led to an increase in local consumption demand, thus creating a new growth point for GHG emissions (Qu 2013). Meanwhile, as the number of meat sheep increased, the total household income increased, with a positive correlation (R2 = 0.59, P 0.001). A positive correlation was also found that between the number of meat sheep and total household expenditure (R2 = 0.10, P 0.001) (Fig. S1). There was a close relationship between meat sheep production and household livelihood components, and understanding the interde- pendence between these two components might help to reduce emis- sions from household ranches.

Our SEM results indicated that the economy was the primary driver of household GHG emissions and household income was the major in- fluence on the economic level. This was in line with the findings of Liu and Zhang (2022), who investigated the carbon footprint of household consumption across provinces of China and hypothesized that increasing income levels further increase the carbon footprint. Dong and Zhao (2017) also pointed out that in all regions of China except for the western rural areas, per capita income had a positive effect on per capita CO2 emissions. Moreover, several studies revealed a strong relationship between household income and carbon emissions. For instance, Seriño and Klasen (2015) found positive effect between household income and household carbon emissions.

In addition, this study demonstrated that the number of sheep showed a significant impact on GHG emissions. With the implementa- tion of grassland protection policies and the improved of sustainable development, the environmental protection awareness of pastoralists had increased. However, economic interests still drive pastoralists to increase the number of meat sheep for higher income, which led to increased GHG emissions and other ecological problems such as grass- land degradation. The government could play a role in reduction of GHG emissions from household ranches by establishing a balance between grassland and livestock populations. This might be achieved by deter- mining a reasonable carrying capacity for livestock and implementation of grassland protection policies. In the process of meat sheep breeding, pastoralists should improve the quality of meat sheep breeding, scien- tific breeding, and enhance meat sheep production management so that the same number of meat sheep could produce higher production value.

45: <header>4.3. Recommendations to reduce meadow steppe GHG emissions in the future </header>

It was observed that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production on household ranches accounted for 67.52 and it was important to explore how to conduct meat sheep production to reduce emissions in this study area. On the other hand, it is necessary to strengthen research on meat goat production technology and limit the growth of livestock numbers by optimizing the livestock structure and improving the effi- ciency of meat sheep production (Hedenus et al., 2014). The improved genetics, animal health, nutrition, and modern reproductive manage- ment could be combined to promote reproductive efficiency, which raised the productive lifespan of meat sheep and reduced their mortality (Herrero et al., 2015). Besides, GHG emissions from meat sheep pro- duction could be reduced by properly managing ranches to increase carbon sequestration, optimizing ranches resources, and implementing an efficient grazing system.

In addition to controlling the unrestricted growth of meat sheep numbers, providing methane emission reduction programs with tangible benefits was imperative to achieving emission reductions in the live- stock sector. Enteric methane fermentation was controlled by three key factors, including the rate of organic matter (OM) fermentation, the type of volatile fatty acids (VFA) produced, and the efficiency of microbial biosynthesis (Wei et al., 2018). The amount and quality of feed ingested control the degree of methane generation and emissions. Various studies had been conducted on methane reduction mechanisms in ruminants, including nutrition management, livestock management, and breeding management (Kumar et al., 2014; Kumari et al., 2020). In the selection of forage, the leguminous forages with lower methane emissions could be preferred to grassy forages (Samsudin et al., 2016). Additionally, the ruminal control and the appropriate application of regulators were also important abatement strategies (Knapp al., 2014; Marino et al., 2016). The impact of vaccines on ruminant methane reduction is still debatable, while Subharat et al. (2015) proposed that vaccination with such vac- cines might reduce methane emissions through the induction of anti- bodies in saliva. Goopy (2019) argued that methanogenic bacterial vaccines could reduce enteric methane emissions, but there was currently no concrete evidence to support this claim. According to the findings of Dangal et al. (2017), unless effective solutions were imple- mented to minimize the GHG emissions from the livestock sector, the increasing production of livestock was likely to result in increasing enteric methane emissions in the future.

At present, the development of household energy in pastoral areas still faced some problems, such as excessive reliance on traditional en- ergy for heating, low awareness of clean energy, and low awareness of the health risks associated with burning livestock manure. In this study, coal consumption emissions accounted for the second-highest total emissions from household ranches (20.80 and were also the largest source of household livelihood emissions. Due to its geographic location, the research area experienced an earlier and longer winter, which

increased the usage of coal. A study of the household of Xilinhot pas- toralists of the carbon footprint by Zhao et al. (2019) revealed a similar trend, with coal consumption emissions making up a sizable portion of energy consumption at 79.26 %. Therefore, it was crucial to raise awareness among pastoralists and encourage them to adopt more sus- tainable energy consumption practices, such as reducing the use of traditional energy sources like coal and cattle manure and exploring alternative, renewable energy options like solar and wind power. In addition, promoting low-carbon products and reducing GHG emissions could be achieved by encouraging pastoralists to modify their con- sumption habits and make eco-friendly choices. Furthermore, promot- ing the use of local, renewable energy sources will not only help mitigate GHG emissions but also support the shift towards a more sustainable future.

Different dietary choices had different effects on GHG emissions (Veeramani et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021). Emerging research highlights that dietary choices in today's society exert a significant influence on the environment, health, and food systems (Hallstrom et al., 2015). Food consumption was the second source of household livelihood emissions (23.93%) and 7.73 % of total emissions in this study. Pastoralist areas had relatively high GHG emissions from meat consumption due to their livelihood conditions and dietary habits. Adopting a diet that empha- sizes plant-based foods over animal products could provide more miti- gation opportunities. The GHG intensity per unit of food varied greatly across animal and vegetable categories, making the actual amount of mitigation dependent on the specific food items that were substituted (Berners-Lee et al., 2012; Hedenus et al., 2014). While switching from meat to vegetables might not align with historical and current global trends of rising per capita meat consumption, it was still possible to choose foods from specific food groups that have a smaller carbon footprint.

Food consumption was the second source of household livelihood emissions (23.93%) and 7.73 % of total emissions in this study. Pastoralist areas had relatively high GHG emissions from meat consumption due to their livelihood conditions and dietary habits. Adopting a diet that empha- sizes plant-based foods over animal products could provide more miti- gation opportunities. The GHG intensity per unit of food varied greatly across animal and vegetable categories, making the actual amount of mitigation dependent on the specific food items that were substituted (Berners-Lee et al., 2012; Hedenus et al., 2014). While switching from meat to vegetables might not align with historical and current global trends of rising per capita meat consumption, it was still possible to choose foods from specific food groups that have a smaller carbon footprint.
47: <header>4.3. Recommendations to reduce meadow steppe GHG emissions in the future </header>

It was observed that the GHG emissions from meat sheep production on household ranches accounted for 67.52 and it was important to explore how to conduct meat sheep production to reduce emissions in this study area. On the other hand, it is necessary to strengthen research on meat goat production technology and limit the growth of livestock numbers by optimizing the livestock structure and improving the effi- ciency of meat sheep production (Hedenus et al., 2014). The improved genetics, animal health, nutrition, and modern reproductive manage- ment could be combined to promote reproductive efficiency, which raised the productive lifespan of meat sheep and reduced their mortality (Herrero et al., 2015). Besides, GHG emissions from meat sheep pro- duction could be reduced by properly managing ranches to increase carbon sequestration, optimizing ranches resources, and implementing an efficient grazing system.

In addition to controlling the unrestricted growth of meat sheep numbers, providing methane emission reduction programs with tangible benefits was imperative to achieving emission reductions in the live- stock sector. Enteric methane fermentation was controlled by three key factors, including the rate of organic matter (OM) fermentation, the type of volatile fatty acids (VFA) produced, and the efficiency of microbial biosynthesis (Wei et al., 2018). The amount and quality of feed ingested control the degree of methane generation and emissions. Various studies had been conducted on methane reduction mechanisms in ruminants, including nutrition management, livestock management, and breeding management (Kumar et al., 2014; Kumari et al., 2020). In the selection of forage, the leguminous forages with lower methane emissions could be preferred to grassy forages (Samsudin et al., 2016). Additionally, the ruminal control and the appropriate application of regulators were also important abatement strategies (Knapp al., 2014; Marino et al., 2016). The impact of vaccines on ruminant methane reduction is still debatable, while Subharat et al. (2015) proposed that vaccination with such vac- cines might reduce methane emissions through the induction of anti- bodies in saliva. Goopy (2019) argued that methanogenic bacterial vaccines could reduce enteric methane emissions, but there was currently no concrete evidence to support this claim. According to the findings of Dangal et al. (2017), unless effective solutions were imple- mented to minimize the GHG emissions from the livestock sector, the increasing production of livestock was likely to result in increasing enteric methane emissions in the future.

At present, the development of household energy in pastoral areas still faced some problems, such as excessive reliance on traditional en- ergy for heating, low awareness of clean energy, and low awareness of the health risks associated with burning livestock manure. In this study, coal consumption emissions accounted for the second-highest total emissions from household ranches (20.80 and were also the largest source of household livelihood emissions. Due to its geographic location, the research area experienced an earlier and longer winter, which

increased the usage of coal. A study of the household of Xilinhot pas- toralists of the carbon footprint by Zhao et al. (2019) revealed a similar trend, with coal consumption emissions making up a sizable portion of energy consumption at 79.26 %. Therefore, it was crucial to raise awareness among pastoralists and encourage them to adopt more sus- tainable energy consumption practices, such as reducing the use of traditional energy sources like coal and cattle manure and exploring alternative, renewable energy options like solar and wind power. In addition, promoting low-carbon products and reducing GHG emissions could be achieved by encouraging pastoralists to modify their con- sumption habits and make eco-friendly choices. Furthermore, promot- ing the use of local, renewable energy sources will not only help mitigate GHG emissions but also support the shift towards a more sustainable future.

Different dietary choices had different effects on GHG emissions (Veeramani et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021). Emerging research highlights that dietary choices in today's society exert a significant influence on the environment, health, and food systems (Hallstrom et al., 2015). Food consumption was the second source of household livelihood emissions (23.93%) and 7.73 % of total emissions in this study. Pastoralist areas had relatively high GHG emissions from meat consumption due to their livelihood conditions and dietary habits. Adopting a diet that empha- sizes plant-based foods over animal products could provide more miti- gation opportunities. The GHG intensity per unit of food varied greatly across animal and vegetable categories, making the actual amount of mitigation dependent on the specific food items that were substituted (Berners-Lee et al., 2012; Hedenus et al., 2014). While switching from meat to vegetables might not align with historical and current global trends of rising per capita meat consumption, it was still possible to choose foods from specific food groups that have a smaller carbon footprint.

50: Based on household survey data of Hulunber, we combined LCA and SEM methods to comprehensively assess GHG emissions from household ranches in the Eurasian meadow steppe, which mainly consisted of two components: livestock production and household livelihood consump- tion. However, there were several limitations to our research. Firstly, the limitations on the raw data were caused by the fact that the household business data recalled by the herders during the survey process may have some errors with the actual data of that year. Nevertheless, the collection of microdata (e.g., household expenditures) was subjective to measurement error, which was a common challenge in questionnaire surveys. Second, as only 160 households were included in our survey involved in Hulunber, although we selected a more representative sample during our research, it might not be able to fully reflect the real situation of the Eurasian meadow steppe, and the number of household ranches would have some influence on the results. To provide a more reliable and general picture of GHG emissions from household ranches in the Eurasian meadow steppe, more households would need to be investigated. In this study, a complete methodology for assessing GHG emissions from household ranches has been well developed, and although there are some limitations, it is still possible to try to apply the methodology to other pastoral areas in Inner Mongolia, China, or other geographical areas facing the same problems.
51: We integrated meat sheep production with household livelihoods and used LCA to calculate and analyze the GHG emissions from house- hold ranches. The results could be significant in that the sheep enteric methane accounted for half of the household GHG emissions, followed by coal use and manure management systems. We also revealed that the GHG emissions were 23.54 kg CO2-eq/kg of meat sheep live weight produced, and the GHG emissions from meat sheep production were

double than of household livelihood. The SEM results indicated that the economic level was the primary driver of GHG emissions of household ranches. The results of regression analysis showed that the number of meat sheep had a significant effect on GHG from household ranches. This study provided valuable information for a general understanding of the interactions between the production and livelihood of local pasto- ralists and helped to establish the potential mitigation options for the sustainable development of grassland resources in Eurasian meadow steppe. Moreover, the emission factors for meat sheep were calculated using region-specific parameters, which could better reflect the real characteristics of emissions in China, but there were uncertainties in both emission factors and activity data. In future research work, we should study more practical methods of reducing livestock enteric methane emissions, improving livestock farming efficiency, and exploring low-carbon grassland household ranches business models. In addition, the establishment of a localized database of emission factors is conducive to a more accurate assessment of livestock production emis- sions in China.
